title: Interview With 'Get Out' Director Jordan Peele | The Big Picture (Ep. 6)
format: Sean Fennessey talks to Jordan Peele about Get Out, its 70s horror touchstones, and the darker ending he cut.

films.title: Get Out
films.note: Peele's directorial debut, out about ten days and past $75 million when he sat down. He outlined it from 2008 to 2012, wrote the script around 2014, and shot it in late 2015 and early 2016 with Blumhouse and QC Entertainment. It started as a story about being the outsider at a party before becoming a horror movie about the African-American experience. He built in five twists on purpose and wrote several endings, including darker ones where the arriving cops end things badly, before settling on Chris escaping.

films.title: Rosemary's Baby
films.note: The vein Peele places the film in, a horror movie working on society rather than monsters.

films.title: The Stepford Wives
films.note: A tonal model. Peele points to how the wives talk like they're in a Clorox ad, a dark satire that stays funny in a subtle way.

films.title: Scream
films.note: Its horror-aware, postmodern streak shaped the character Rod, who says what the audience wishes someone would say without breaking the reality.

films.title: The Shining
films.note: Peele's benchmark for restraint, one jump scare across an entire creepy movie, though he allowed himself a couple.

films.title: Funny Games
films.note: On set, deciding Jeremy's weapon, he ruled out a golf club because Funny Games had already used one and went with a lacrosse stick.

films.title: Sicario
films.note: Where Sean had seen star Daniel Kaluuya, alongside Black Mirror.

films.title: Dirty Dancing
films.note: Its soundtrack turns up in the movie, one of the needle drops the two of them trade notes on.
